summary:
The Seated Woman of Catalhoyuk is a baked clay figurine found at the [[event:11|Catalhoyuk settlement]] in south-central Anatolia, dating to approximately 6000 BCE. The figurine depicts a large, seated female figure whose arms rest on two feline animals, interpreted as leopards or lions. It is one of the most famous Neolithic sculptures in the world and has been widely associated with the concept of a mother goddess or female deity.

The figurine was discovered in a grain bin, a context that has led some scholars to interpret it as a protective or fertility figure associated with agricultural abundance. However, the goddess interpretation has been contested by some researchers who argue that female figurines at Catalhoyuk served a variety of social purposes that need not be reduced to a single religious archetype. Regardless of its precise meaning, the Seated Woman represents the sophistication of symbolic and artistic production at one of the ancient world's largest Neolithic settlements.

images.caption: Çatalhöyük oturan ilahə fiqurunun öndən görünüşü, bişmiş torpaq, e.ə. 5750-ci il
